literal: wrongly, falsely; an indeclinable used adjectivally
invariant_definition: There is no shared invariant — the term is the explicit fault-line between Advaita and its critics. In Advaita technical usage, *mithyā* is a precisely-defined ontological status: *sad-asad-vilakṣaṇa* (different from both *sat* and *asat*), *anirvacanīya* (indeterminable on the binary), *trikāla-abādhya-pratītika-viṣaya* (the object of a cognition not sublated in any of the three times). For non-Advaita schools, *mithyā* is either a polemical target (Dvaita) or a category that does not apply at all to the world (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Bhedābheda, Acintya-Bhedābheda — all of which hold the world to be real, *satya*, not *mithyā*). What is shared: *mithyā ≠ asat*. Even Advaita explicitly insists *mithyā* is not 'non-existent.'

per_school.definition: *Mithyā* is the technical name for the ontological status of the world: neither *sat* (real in the way *brahman* is) nor *asat* (sheer non-existence like the son of a barren woman), but a third status, *anirvacanīya*. The world is *mithyā* because it is sublatable in *brahma-jñāna* (*pratiti-trikāla-bādhya*) yet appears with *vyāvahārika* force throughout transactional life. Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* mounts the defense of *mithyātva* against the Mādhva *Nyāyāmṛta*. Five definitions of *mithyātva* are systematically defended.

The world is *satya*, not *mithyā*. The Advaita category of *anirvacanīya* is rejected as logically incoherent — to be is to be characterizable; *anirvacanīya* is *avastu*. Rāmānuja's *Mahāsiddhānta* in the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* contains the *sapta-anupapatti* (seven untenabilities) against the Advaita doctrine; the third specifically attacks the coherence of *mithyātva*.

*Mithyātva* is the principal target of Dvaita's polemical apparatus. Madhva's short polemics (*Mithyātva-Anumāna-Khaṇḍana*, *Upādhi-Khaṇḍana*, *Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana*) and Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta* construct sustained attacks on every Advaita formulation of *mithyātva*. The world is *satya* in the only sense relevant: it is what Viṣṇu created and sustains, with eternally distinct dependent realities and irreducible difference.

The world is real (*satya*) as the manifestation of Kṛṣṇa's *māyā-śakti*; *mithyātva* in the Advaita sense is rejected. The phenomenal status of the world for the conditioned *jīva* is one of *vaikuṇṭha*-occlusion, not of ontological deficiency.

translator_note: *Mithyā* must NOT be translated as 'unreal' or 'illusory' or 'non-existent.' Each translation collapses the technical distinction Advaita is at pains to make: *mithyā ≠ asat*. The world for Advaita is *sublatable*, not non-existent — it has *vyāvahārika* (transactional) reality and is *anirvacanīya*. The user's position-brief explicitly identifies the conflation '*mithyā* = *asat*' as a fundamental error that has done generational damage to Western reception of Vedānta. Acceptable English glosses: 'sublatable', 'apparently real but not in the way Brahman is real', or simply leaving the Sanskrit untranslated.
